frameworks.name: Mental Accounting and Prepaid Cost Absorption
frameworks.definition: Costs that are paid upfront and mentally committed (stored value cards, advance hotel/airline payments) feel like sunk costs, making subsequent expenditures feel smaller than their absolute price.

claims.text: Cost is a feeling, not a number. When a customer has prepaid or committed funds (e.g., $30 on a Starbucks Stored Value Card), spending $20 from that pool feels like a $10 expense because the prepaid amount is mentally written off as sunk cost.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Mental accounting treats prepaid funds as already spent; drawing from the pool feels like reducing loss, not incurring new cost

claims.text: Approximately $1 billion in Starbucks Stored Value funds sit unused globally at any moment, indicating the psychological power of prepayment to decouple actual spending from perceived cost.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

numbers.axis: prepaid funds globally held across all Starbucks cards

claims.text: When customers have paid for the base product or service upfront (airline seat, hotel room), they mentally write off that cost as sunk. Subsequent upsell offers (upgrades) are evaluated only against the marginal upgrade price, not the total cost.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: upsell or upgrade offer presented at time of service
claims.mechanism: Sunk cost mental accounting causes customers to ignore the base payment and evaluate only the marginal cost, making upgrades appear cheaper than they would if evaluated as total incremental spend
bounded_by: Does not apply when customer has paid at time of service (no sunk-cost decoupling)
Does not apply to low-price impulse purchases where mental accounting is minimal

claims.text: As a pricing strategy, make customers pay twice: once upfront (via prepayment, stored value, or deposit) and again at the time of service. This separation makes the second payment feel smaller.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: customer willingness to prepay or deposit
service or product with deferred consumption
claims.mechanism: Double payment architecture leverages mental accounting; the upfront commitment is mentally separated from consumption-time expenditure, reducing perceived total cost
bounded_by: Does not apply in low-trust contexts where prepayment creates friction
Does not apply to subscription models where payment is continuous

examples.text: A customer with $30 on a Starbucks Stored Value Card spends $20 on coffee. Because they have mentally written off the $30 as already committed to Starbucks, the $20 transaction feels like only a $10 expense (the reduction in their remaining balance), not a full $20 cost.

An airline passenger who purchased a ticket 3 months ago for $400 is offered a $150 seat upgrade at check-in. Because they have mentally written off the original $400 ticket cost, the upgrade offer is evaluated only against the $150 price, making it feel like a marginal $150 addition rather than a $550 total spend.

A hotel guest who booked and paid for a room 3 months earlier is offered a suite upgrade for $80 at check-in. The guest mentally separates the upfront payment (written off as sunk cost) from the upgrade offer, evaluating the upgrade only on its $80 marginal cost, not the true incremental total.

practices.text: Collect payment for the core product or service upfront and as far in advance as possible. This front-loads the sunk cost and creates mental separation between the base investment and any subsequent upsells or upgrades.
When presenting upsells or upgrades, show only the incremental price, not the total cost. Customers who have prepaid will naturally anchor to the marginal cost.
